title: Activation Functions
description: Ten activation functions with formulas, derivatives, SVGs, TF code, pros/cons, and FAANG insights — from classic sigmoid to modern GELU and SwiGLU.
details: Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex patterns. The choice of activation determines gradient flow, training speed, and expressivity. Modern deep learning favors smooth non-saturating functions like GELU and Swish, while classic functions like sigmoid and tanh remain useful for output layers and gating mechanisms.

sections.title: Sigmoid / Logistic Function
sections.description:
Formula & Derivative

σ(x) = 1 / (1 + e⁻ˣ)
σ′(x) = σ(x)(1 - σ(x))   ← max = 0.25 at x=0

Range: (0, 1) — outputs as probabilities
Key failure: Saturation → vanishing gradient. Max derivative is 0.25. For a 10-layer net, best-case gradient is 0.25¹⁰ ≈ 10⁻⁶ — learning stops.

[Intuition]

Sigmoid squashes any real number into a probability-like range. The problem is the flat tails — once |x| > 4, the derivative is essentially 0, so the neuron stops learning. Modern networks avoid sigmoid everywhere except the final output layer (binary classification).

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth, differentiable, outputs in (0,1) for probability interpretation
Cons: Vanishing gradient for |x|>4, not zero-centered (outputs all positive → zigzag gradient updates), computationally expensive exp()

When to Use

Binary classification output layer. Avoid in hidden layers entirely.

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "Why does sigmoid cause vanishing gradients?"
A: The max derivative is 0.25 at x=0. For a 10-layer network with sigmoid activations, the backpropagated gradient is at most 0.25¹⁰ ≈ 10⁻⁶ — effectively zero. Compare: ReLU's derivative is 1 for x > 0, so gradients flow freely.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: sigmoid activation
# Use for binary classification output layer

model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')  # output in (0, 1)
])

tf.keras.activations.sigmoid(x)  # standalone call

sections.title: Tanh — Zero-Centered Saturation
sections.description:
Formula & Derivative

tanh(x) = (eˣ − e⁻ˣ) / (eˣ + e⁻ˣ)
tanh′(x) = 1 − tanh²(x)

Range: (−1, 1) — zero-centered
Key advantage: Zero-centered means gradients don't push all weights in the same direction (unlike sigmoid). Still saturates at extremes.

[Intuition]

"Zero-centered means the gradient doesn't push all weights in the same direction." Tanh outputs range -1 to 1, so the average activation can be 0. This makes it strictly better than sigmoid for hidden layers, though ReLU and its variants have largely replaced both.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Zero-centered (better gradient dynamics than sigmoid), saturating behavior useful for gating mechanisms in LSTMs
Cons: Still saturates → vanishing gradient at extremes, more expensive than ReLU

When to Use

LSTM/GRU gates (input gate, forget gate, output gate all use tanh or sigmoid), older RNN architectures.

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "Why is tanh preferred over sigmoid for hidden layers?"
A: Tanh is zero-centered (−1 to 1), which means the gradient of the loss w.r.t. weights below doesn't have a biased direction. With sigmoid (0 to 1), all weight updates move in the same sign direction, causing inefficient zigzag optimization.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: tanh activation

model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='tanh'),  # hidden layer
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])

tf.keras.activations.tanh(x)  # standalone call

sections.title: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)
sections.description:
Formula & Derivative

ReLU(x) = max(0, x)

ReLU′(x) = 0  for x < 0
ReLU′(x) = 1  for x > 0
(derivative undefined at x=0 — subgradient: [0, 1])

Range: [0, ∞)
Key insight: Non-saturating for x > 0 (constant gradient = 1 → no vanishing gradient). The non-linearity comes from the hinge at 0.

[Intuition]

"All the benefits of linearity for x > 0 (constant gradient = 1) with just enough non-linearity from the hinge at 0." The negative region being exactly 0 means ReLU creates sparse activations — for a given input, roughly 50% of neurons output exactly 0. This sparsity is computationally efficient.

Pros & Cons

Pros: No vanishing gradient for x>0, computationally cheap (just max), induces sparsity, empirically works well
Cons: Dead ReLU problem (neuron stuck at 0, never recovers), not differentiable at 0, outputs not zero-centered
Dead ReLU fix: Use Leaky ReLU, PReLU, or careful initialization — He init specifically designed for ReLU: Var[W] = 2/fan_in

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "Derive He initialization for ReLU networks."
A: For ReLU, half the neurons are dead (output 0) on average, so effective fan_in is halved. We want Var[yₗ] = Var[yₗ₋₁] to prevent signal explosion/vanishing. This requires Var[W] = 2/fan_in. For Xavier (sigmoid/tanh), Var[W] = 2/(fan_in + fan_out). He init doubles Xavier's variance to compensate for the 50% activation rate.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: ReLU activation

model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])

tf.keras.activations.relu(x)                      # standalone call
tf.keras.layers.ReLU(max_value=6.0)               # ReLU6 variant (MobileNet)
tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='relu')      # as activation string

sections.title: Leaky ReLU & Parametric ReLU (PReLU)
sections.description:
Formula

LReLU(x) = max(αx, x)    where α = 0.01 (typical)
PReLU(x) = max(αᵢx, x)  where αᵢ is learned per channel

α: 0.01 for Leaky ReLU, learned for PReLU
Key insight: Small negative gradient lets dead neurons recover. PReLU learns α per channel — useful with enough data but adds model parameters.

[Intuition]

"A small gradient in the negative region lets dead neurons recover." The asymmetry (small negative slope, steep positive slope) preserves the sparsity benefits of ReLU while giving a second chance to neurons that would otherwise be dead permanently.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Fixes dead ReLU, simple modification, PReLU adds expressivity
Cons: PReLU adds parameters (one α per channel), inconsistent α values during training, marginal improvement over ReLU in practice

When to Use

When dead ReLU is observed (e.g., large model, long training, poor initialization). PReLU for large-scale CV models with sufficient data.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: Leaky ReLU and PReLU

# Leaky ReLU with fixed alpha=0.01
model.add(tf.keras.layers.LeakyReLU(alpha=0.01))

# PReLU with learned alpha (one scalar, or per channel)
model.add(tf.keras.layers.PReLU(shared_axes=[1, 2]))  # per-channel for Conv2D

sections.title: ELU (Exponential Linear Unit)
sections.description:
Formula

ELU(x) = x             for x ≥ 0
ELU(x) = α(eˣ − 1)    for x < 0    (α = 1 typically)

Range: (−α, ∞) — saturates to −α for large negative x
Key insight: Smooth saturation near 0 (unlike ReLU's sharp corner). Negative values push mean activation toward 0 → speeds up learning. Clevert et al. showed ELU can replace BatchNorm in some cases.

[Intuition]

ELU has a smooth bend at x=0 rather than ReLU's sharp kink. This makes gradient-based optimization easier (smooth loss landscape). The negative saturation means the average activation drifts toward 0, which reduces bias shift.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth at 0, negative saturation pushes mean activation toward 0, robust to noise
Cons: Expensive exp() computation, saturates for large negative values (can still cause gradient issues)

When to Use

Alternate to ReLU when computational cost isn't a concern. SELU (self-normalizing variant, α≈1.673, λ≈1.05) for self-normalizing networks.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: ELU and SELU

model.add(tf.keras.layers.ELU(alpha=1.0))

# SELU — self-normalizing variant (requires LecunNormal init + AlphaDropout)
model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, kernel_initializer='lecun_normal'))
model.add(tf.keras.layers.SELU())

sections.title: Swish / SiLU (Sigmoid Linear Unit)
sections.description:
Formula

Swish(x) = x · σ(x) = x / (1 + e⁻ˣ)
SiLU(x)  = x · σ(x)  ← same function, discovered independently

Key property: Self-gated (input times its own sigmoid). Smooth, non-monotonic — dips below 0 near x ≈ −1.3, creating a "soft floor."

[Intuition]

"Unlike ReLU which has a hard cutoff at 0, Swish smoothly dips below 0, creating a 'soft floor'." This non-monotonicity allows the function to better capture complex decision boundaries. Discovered by Ramachandran et al. (2017) via neural architecture search — it beats ReLU on ImageNet.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth everywhere (better gradient flow), non-monotonic, found via AutoML to outperform ReLU
Cons: More expensive than ReLU (requires sigmoid), no sparsity (no exact zeros)

When to Use

Used in EfficientNet, MobileNetV3 (hard-swish variant). Good default for modern architectures when compute budget allows.

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "Why is Swish considered a self-gated activation?"
A: Swish(x) = x · σ(x). The sigmoid σ(x) acts as a gate between 0 and 1 that controls how much of x flows through. For x ≪ 0, the gate is ~0, for x ≫ 0, the gate is ~1. This is analogous to the input gate in LSTMs — the activation value gates itself.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: Swish / SiLU activation

model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(128, activation='swish'))

# Or use SiLU (identical):
tf.keras.activations.swish(x)

# Hard Swish (for mobile):
# h-swish(x) = x · ReLU6(x+3) / 6
# Used in MobileNetV3 for speed on mobile hardware

sections.title: GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit)
sections.description:
Formula

GELU(x) = x · Φ(x)        ← Φ(x) = P(X ≤ x) for X ∼ N(0, 1)
       ≈ 0.5x(1 + tanh(√(2/π)(x + 0.044715x³)))  ← tanh approximation

Range: ≈ (−0.17, ∞) — slightly negative for x < 0
Key insight: Dropout + ReLU combined = GELU. The function stochastically applies dropout-like attenuation to negative inputs. Used in BERT, GPT-3, GPT-4, LLaMA, ViT.

[Intuition]

"Think of it as applying dropout to inputs based on how far below 0 they are — bigger negatives get dropped more." The Gaussian CDF Φ(x) means for x = −2, GELU outputs approximately 0, while for x = 0, GELU(x) = 0 — exactly halfway between being kept and dropped.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth gradient (no hard kink), stochastic regularization effect, non-monotonic, gradient doesn't saturate for negative
Cons: More expensive than ReLU (requires tanh approximation), no sparsity

When to Use

Default for Transformer/LLM architectures. LLaMA uses GELU in its FFN blocks. Replaced ReLU in all GPT iterations after GPT-1.

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "Why does GPT use GELU?" — OpenAI L5
A: GELU gives a smoother gradient than ReLU, which is helpful at large scale. The smooth curvature prevents the hard kink at 0 from creating pathological loss landscapes. Additionally, GELU's connection to stochastic regularization (Dropout) means it naturally provides regularization without extra hyperparameters. At GPT-3's 175B scale, every percentage point of training stability matters.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: GELU activation

model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(512, activation='gelu'))

tf.keras.activations.gelu(x, approximate=True)   # tanh approximation
tf.keras.activations.gelu(x, approximate=False)  # exact via erf

# GELU has largely replaced ReLU in LLMs:
# BERT: GELU | GPT-3: GELU | LLaMA: SwiGLU (GELU variant)

sections.title: Mish — Self-Gated Smooth Activation
sections.description:
Formula

Mish(x) = x · tanh(softplus(x))
        = x · tanh(ln(1 + eˣ))

Key property: Self-gated via tanh(softplus(x)). Smooth, non-monotonic like Swish, with marginal empirical improvements.

[Intuition]

Mish = x · tanh(softplus(x)). The softplus creates a smooth version of ReLU, then tanh squashes it to (−1, 1), and x re-scales. The result is a smooth Swish-like curve with a slightly different shape (the dip below 0 is shallower).

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth everywhere, non-monotonic, self-gated
Cons: More expensive than Swish (needs softplus + tanh), no theoretical breakthrough, minor gains over Swish

When to Use

YOLOv4 uses Mish. Worth trying as a Swish replacement in CV architectures. Not widely adopted in NLP/LLMs.

sections.title: Softmax — Probability Distribution Output
sections.description:
Formula

σ(z)ᵢ = e^(zᵢ / T) / Σⱼ e^(zⱼ / T)

T = temperature (T < 1 → peaky, T > 1 → smooth)

Range: (0, 1), sums to 1 — valid probability distribution
Key insight: Softmax amplifies differences. For logits [5, 1, −2], softmax with T=1 gives approximately [0.98, 0.02, 0.001]. Temperature T controls the "peakiness."

[Intuition]

"Exponentiate so all values are positive, normalize so they sum to 1." This is the multi-class generalization of sigmoid. The exponentiation means: big logits become very big, small logits become tiny, and the normalization forces competition between classes.

Numerical Stability

Always subtract max(z) before exponentiation: softmax(z) = e^(z − max(z)) / Σ e^(z − max(z)). Otherwise e^(1000) overflows to Inf.

[Concrete Numbers]

For raw logits [5, 1, −2], after softmax (T=1):
e⁵ = 148.4, e¹ = 2.7, e⁻² = 0.13. Sum = 151.2.
p = [148.4/151.2, 2.7/151.2, 0.13/151.2] ≈ [0.981, 0.018, 0.001]
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: Softmax activation

model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax'))

tf.keras.activations.softmax(x, axis=-1)           # default: last dim
tf.nn.log_softmax(x)                               # log(softmax(x)) for stability

# Temperature scaling:
# lower T = sharper distribution, higher T = softer
softmax_with_temp = tf.keras.activations.softmax(x / 0.5)  # T = 0.5

sections.title: Softplus — Smooth ReLU Approximation
sections.description:
Formula

Softplus(x) = ln(1 + eˣ)

As x → ∞:  ln(1 + eˣ) ≈ x   (linear)
As x → −∞: ln(1 + eˣ) ≈ eˣ  (positive exponential tail)

Range: (0, ∞) — always positive, never exactly zero
Key insight: Smooth everywhere, differentiable at 0 (unlike ReLU). Always positive — never kills the gradient completely. Used in β-VAE for KL divergence parameterization.

[Intuition]

Softplus is what you get when you "soften" ReLU's hinge at 0. It asymptotically approaches ReLU as x → ∞ but has a smooth positive tail for x < 0. This means it never outputs exactly 0 (unlike ReLU) — useful when you need guaranteed positive outputs.

Pros & Cons

Pros: Smooth everywhere, differentiable, always positive, no dead neurons
Cons: Expensive exp() computation, no sparsity (always positive), less used than ReLU variants

When to Use

When you need smooth, positive, differentiable outputs. β-VAE uses softplus for the KL divergence standard deviation parameterization. Also used as a component in Mish.
sections.codeBlock:
# TensorFlow: Softplus activation

model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dense(64, activation='softplus'))

tf.keras.activations.softplus(x)

# Common use: parameterizing positive values
log_var = tf.keras.layers.Dense(10)(x)
var = tf.keras.activations.softplus(log_var) + 1e-6  # guaranteed > 0

sections.title: Comparison Table & Summary
sections.description:
FunctionRangeGrad RangeZero-CtrMonoCostFamous ModelsDead Risk
Sigmoid(0, 1)[0, 0.25]NoYesMedLogistic RegressionLow
Tanh(−1, 1)[0, 1]YesYesMedLSTM gatesLow
ReLU[0, ∞){0, 1}NoYesLowResNet, VGG, AlexNetHigh
Leaky ReLU(−∞, ∞){α, 1}NoYesLowGAN discriminatorLow
ELU(−α, ∞)(0, 1]Near-0YesHighSELU variantsLow
Swish/SiLU(−0.28, ∞)[−0.1, 1.1]NoNoHighEfficientNet, MobileNetLow
GELU(−0.17, ∞)[0, ~1.1]NoNoHighGPT-3/4, BERT, ViTLow
Mish(−0.31, ∞)[−0.1, 1.1]NoNoHighYOLOv4Low
Softmax(0, 1), Σ=1dependsN/AN/AMedAll classifiersN/A
Softplus(0, ∞)(0, 1)NoYesHighβ-VAENone

Key takeaway: The industry trend is: ReLU → GELU → SwiGLU. ReLU for speed and sparsity, GELU for smooth gradients at scale, SwiGLU for maximum expressivity in LLMs. The loss landscape benefits of smooth activations (GELU, Swish) outweigh their computational cost at large scale.

[Practical Workflow] Practical Workflow — Choosing an Activation Function:

  1. Default choice: ReLU for hidden layers in most feedforward/CNN models
  2. If dead ReLU is observed (no activations > 0 in a layer), try Leaky ReLU or ELU
  3. For Transformers/LLMs: GELU (standard in GPT, BERT, LLaMA)
  4. For output layer: sigmoid (binary), softmax (multiclass), linear (regression)
  5. For RNNs: tanh (default), ReLU with careful init

[Interview Angle] FAANG Interview Question

Q: "If you had to pick one activation for a new deep architecture, which would you choose?"
A: GELU, unless compute constraints are severe. It's smooth (better gradients), has a stochastic regularization effect, and is the industry consensus default (used in GPT, BERT, ViT). For mobile/edge, ReLU or Hard Swish. For LLMs specifically, SwiGLU (LLaMA, PaLM).

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Ramachandran, P., et al. "Searching for Activation Functions." (Swish) — 2017
  • Hendrycks, D. & Gimpel, K. "Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs)." — 2016
  • Clevert, D., et al. "Fast and Accurate Deep Network Learning by Exponential Linear Units (ELUs)." — 2015
  • He, K., et al. "Delving Deep into Rectifiers: Surpassing Human-Level Performance on ImageNet Classification." (PReLU, He init) — 2015
  • Maas, A., et al. "Rectifier Nonlinearities Improve Neural Network Acoustic Models." (Leaky ReLU) — 2013
  • Misra, D. "Mish: A Self Regularized Non-Monotonic Neural Activation Function." — 2019
  • Nair, V. & Hinton, G. "Rectified Linear Units Improve Restricted Boltzmann Machines." (ReLU) — 2010
  • CS231n Lecture Notes — Activation Functions. Stanford.
